data.title: Burford Capital - We'll Have To See How Bad This Gets
data.description:
A dead cat bounce is possible

Burford Capital (BUR) has something of a problem here. The big issue to be faced by us out here, as investors or potential such, is how much of a problem? This is something not wholly knowable at present which makes a valuation difficult. It's possible to have a view, of course, but I am not sure how analytically correct such a view is going to be.

Essentially, the difficulty is in working out how large a disaster this loss on the YPF/Repsol case is going to be. That in turn will influence our thinking on whether we might see a recovery in the price from Friday's collapse or, instead, it's going to get worse.

That, in my view at least, depends upon what we think they're going to do about past revenue from this case they've already recognised in this case.

The background here

As I've said before about Burford:

Burford actually has a nice business here. Financing litigation and then gaining some portion of the award for having done so. I know they'll hate the idea of being compared to ambulance chasers - for they are not ambulance chasers - but the economic model is the same. Burford fund litigation then gain a portion of the award for having done so. As at the top this is a valuable economic service.

The question is always well, how much will the award be? And will it get paid?

The basic idea is just fine as a concept. Burford have also had a pipeline of cases, won some of them and thus made profits on those cases. None of this is contested.

But we've also a conceptual problem with the accounts of any business doing something like this:

One of these companies, Burford Capital (BUR) wandered into problems in this field some years back. I've had the occasional email from them, in fact, they are muttering that perhaps I'm not being wholly fair to them. No, that wasn't about my coverage at Seeking Alpha, this is elsewhere.

The problem was - and at this distance we'd not say that anyone was wrong, just that there was a difference of opinion - that if you've some process that takes many years, then, well, when do you claim income? There was a short selling report that worried about Burford. That report here, me on that report here. Burford stoutly denied this. It took some time for the market more generally to believe Burford on this - to again point to opinion, not fact. In this now long-term, everyone in the market thinks this is not a problem now.

In more detail, clearly if you're in a 5-year process, then you don't claim all the income in year 5 when you actually get the check. Equally, clearly, you don't claim all the income you *might* get in year one. There has to be a system of release of likely earnings - winnings, legal fees, whatever - from the reserve of what might happen to likely will happen across the P&L over time.

How much and when, well, differences of opinion.

We're now back with exactly that problem. What revenue, recognised when? That's the issue here and one that colours - or at least darn well should colour - anyone's view of the stock.

Let's just stipulate

Just for ease of analysis - or, if you prefer, to concentrate on the major issue here - let's all agree that the general business at Burford is just fine. They collect cases, they finance them, some - enough - pay out that the business chugs along. The one and only issue we've got with the current valuation is the Argentine case.

The Argentina case

This is a truly vast case, the judgement worth $16 billion and change. Some of that has been sold on to other investors, some remains on the books. Leaving aside who owns how much of it, investors or the company, total revenue should be 70% or so of that judgment. This is such a large sum that yes, we can - to gain a useful view at least - consider that this is the business itself. To repeat, it's not all of the business but as a valuation technique this one case can be studied.

The background is that Argentina nationalised, or expropriated, YPF, which was the local Argentine subsidiary of Repsol at the time. The claim has long been that the country undercompensated for that expropriation and therefore compensation was due.

The problem here is that recognition of income. For there was that judgment for $16 billion. Which appeared in Burford's accounts - as it should have done. You've got a judgment in your favour, this should appear in your accounts. But the appeals court has just reversed that judgment.

Burford Capital (BUR) sank 40% after Argentina won a reversal of a decision in a YPF (YPF) case on appeal. YPF ADRs rose 3.7%.Argentina had a US court decision against it over the 2012 seizure of YPF overturned on appeal, a federal appeals court said on Friday in its ruling. The lawsuit was backed by litigation funder Burford (BUR).

Well, yes, losing that sort of $16 billion of potential future income will have an effect upon a stock price:

I've given the full decade there because that shows us the peak of that price. Back before that Muddy Waters short selling report. If we want the reaction just to the latest news:

A distinct revaluation of the company. As we'd expect from the loss of $16 billion in potential future revenue of course.

But that's not enough

That's not enough for analysis that is. Burford itself says they're going to update us all right soon now:

Burford's management and board of directors, along with Burford's valuation committee and its external auditors, will consider the financial impact of the decision in connection with Burford's first quarter reporting. Burford's valuation policy calls for a partial write-down of assets following an intermediate appellate loss, and we would expect such a non-cash write-down to occur here, although we have not yet determined its magnitude and will include those details as part of our first quarter reporting which we would normally release in early May.

Given the substantial carrying value of the YPF matter on Burford's balance sheet, a material write-down could reduce Burford's balance sheet equity value below the level required under the indentures governing our senior notes to incur additional debt under the provisions permitting debt incurrences based on our debt to equity ratio, which would limit Burford's ability to issue new debt. Our ability to make restricted payments or permitted investments based on our debt to equity ratio also could be limited. Burford no longer has any outstanding debt with maintenance financial covenants that would be implicated by a decline in balance sheet equity.

So, yes, this has substantial impact.

The decision itself is here. Of course this is appealable again, en banc to the same appeals court, then onto the US Supreme. And in the judgment itself it is noted that the entire issue could be run again through ISDS - the international treaties covering foreign investors and so on. The current judgment is that it's not justiciable under Argentine law, not that the case has no merit under any alternative system of legal fight.

But here's the big question

To go back to those original Muddy Waters claims:

What's really being alleged here is that Burford has been over valuing the claims still in the pipeline. It's possible, in theory, for that not to be a particular problem. So, we all just have to wait a little longer to find out.

Muddy Waters is going further than that. They're saying that Burford has been declaring profits as a result of these over valuations. And paying dividends on those over valuations. Even, paying management significant amounts based upon those valuations that aren't going to come true.

If that is so then the stock is obviously worth much less than the market currently thinks. Well, obviously, if recovery is going to be less than we all think then it's worth less.

And as I've already noted, of course some sort of weighting, probability, has to be applied to the pipeline of future potential revenues. It would be absurd - and wholly misleading to investors - to either book all revenue when just starting a case or all revenue only upon receipt. Some estimate of likelihood has to be made.

But this YPF case is so large, and has been going on for so long, that substantial revenue has been booked from it already. So, how much claimed past revenue is going to need to be reversed? That's what the valuation uncertainty is now. And it's a biggie too.

From the most recent accounts:

Revenues 413,360

Fair value adjustment during the period from YPF-related assets 272,043

Oh. No, this is right, it's the correct approach. That YPF payout was getting so close it was possible to taste it. It should have some amount reflected in the accounts. But, umm, the amount recognised was 65% of revenues for the year. Which is an ouchie really, isn't it?

2024:

Unrealized gains/(losses) from YPF-related assets 127,162

That's of $540 million in total revenues so while still a large sum less of a portion.

In 2023 and 2022 I can't see them pulling out the YPF line. That might just be me and my ability to read a set of accounts of course, but they're not doing so in the main P&L section. It could also be that the case was not as settled as it was in the subsequent two years.

Yes, of course there's that problem of the reversal of the judgment. But that problem then comes in three parts.

1) The loss of the potential future income.

2) The reversal of previous fair value adjustments

3) Well, given those reversals, or rather those previous claims of fair value that were booked as revenue, how much is Burford actually worth? Not in the current, balance sheet, sense, but without the YPF adjustments how much profit has it been making and this makes the business worth what?

It's that third that is the truly important matter. If reversing those past bookings wiped out the capital base then Burford would already have told us that. They do say it impacts.

But, without those revenues how much has the company been making? In 2025 that $272m booked was 4.3 times reported profit. In 2024 $127m while profits were $146 million.

Again, as I keep saying, an estimation of future revenue from the YPF case just did have to be made and included in the accounts otherwise it would not have been a fair view. But if those sums are reversed then that's all and more of oorporate profits for the past two years.

Further, as I can't see a breakout of YPF numbers for previous years (as I say, quite possibly just my inability to read the accounts properly) then I don't know how profitable Burford was in earlier years without the bookings from the YPF case.

Which is, as I say, what I think the problem is. Not the writing off of sums previously booked against the balance sheet. But what effect does the revision of those sums over the past then tell us about the profitability of Burford overall?

I don't know therefore I'm not willing to try a valuation.

Why I'm wrong

There's so much up in the air here that of course I could be wrong. Burford might just write off their previously booked revenues and it turns out that everything is fine just in a smaller company. Yes, what we thought were previous revenues aren't but it's no biggie to the feasibility of the company.

It's also possible that that first appeal to a hearing en banc (so the whole appeal court, not just three judges from it) could be accepted and this decision itself reversed. And, obviously, in the long term it's possible that they'll win the case through one or other route. I certainly think they should too but my opinions upon legal matters are not known for their accuracy. Also, that would take some number of years.

My opinion

The uncertainty here is just too large for me to want to play with this ball. We could, wholly reasonably, think that the long term value of the claim is such that we should buy in at some price. It is this version of this case that has been rejected by the court - they openly point out that another route, through international and treaty law could work. 70% of $16 billion is a lot, you know? If Burford writes that all down to nothing on the balance sheet and there's still a viable business with their other cases then that would be a nice option in the background.

Or, the necessity to write off those previously booked sums completely wrecks any estimation of Burford's previous profitablility and so the valuation to be applied to it is pretty minimal. Or, of course, various and varied positions in between those two extremes.

Investing in Burford now would be taking a gamble on where, in that wide space, the result ends up. I take that wide space to extend from well below current levels to considerably above - but not climbing back to the status quo ante of Thursday last week.

In the absence of much better data that's much too much of a gamble for me.

There's also one other wrinkle here. The market price - for the next couple of weeks at least - is not going to be some accurate evaluation of these different possibilities. It's going to be the immediate gut feels of market participants to the news. For a time at least we're not going to be trading against reality but against opinions and emotions fed by uncertainty. Not my game.

data.title: Core Natural Resources: Leverage To Coal/LNG Switching Is Limited
data.description:
Introduction

A few days ago, I analyzed the thermal coal sector, given that it is the only readily available and economically viable substitute for LNG. With about 17% of LNG lost due to the Hormuz Strait closure and, more importantly, about 3% of global supply offline for a few years, it's highly probable that the power generators will switch to thermal coal to first reduce electricity costs and avoid blackouts. Prices have risen to around $130/ton but may move higher as LNG prices rise and approach caloric parity. In that article, my initial sensitivity analysis of Core Natural Resources (CNR) overestimated this year's potential impact due to a high level of already contracted volumes. The company has about 27% of high-quality (CV) coal production available for spot market sale, which limits this year's impact.

Coal Price Parity to LNG

As LNG supplies decrease and prices rise, power plants that use it to generate electricity may first switch to coal to reduce costs, which requires regulatory approval. Under this scenario, coal should trade at a discount to LNG parity. Today, with LNG prices around $20/MTU, high-grade thermal coal’s parity is around $152 on a caloric basis. Many US coal companies have lower quality grades and compete with cheap natural gas. Australia generally has far higher grades and is closer to the Japanese/Korean markets, with lower transport costs. Europe may also switch to coal, but Poland has sufficient supplies to meet demand. Nonetheless, if LNG is in deficit, then coal is the most likely to replace it in electricity generation, and the parity price may be reached.

Limited Leverage to High Prices

After completing the merger with Arch Resources in early 2025, CNR added predominantly Powder River Basin (PRB) coal, which has low energy content and currently sells for $14/ton, compared with over $60/ton for its high-grade calorific value (CV). This acquisition also added metallurgical coal that has an even higher price, $120/ton, but also a higher production cost.

The current consensus forecast calls for EBITDA of $850m in 2026, based on company volume, price, and cost guidance, which is already positive vs. 2025, when metallurgical coal had negative EBITDA. The company stated that 73% of its CV production (32 tons) is contracted at $72/ton ($57 for coal and $15 for transport). This leaves 27% available for the spot market, which is over $130 and rising for 2026. My estimates assume the CV production will be sold at $115, which increases CV EBITDA to $978m, or 68% above 2025. The PRB low-quality coal has been 97% contracted and will not capture any price gains, which I assume will be marginal. Metallurgical coal turns positive with a $133m contribution. My 2026 consolidated EBITDA estimate rises to $1.1bn, or 35% above consensus.

For 2027 and 2028, I assume CV prices stay in the $115 range, but the company is able to capture this in a long-term contract. This scenario has a high probability given the LNG deficit of 3% globally from the destruction of Qatargas capacity that requires 3yrs to repair/rebuild. However, US LNG capacity is rising, and companies such as Venture Global (VG) are adding volumes that could help absorb the shortfall by 2028. Under this section, CNR could see US$2bn in EBITDA before CV coal prices begin to decline.

Valuation

Under a scenario of prolonged LNG deficits through YE28, thermal coal prices should hover over the $150/ton mark. The CNR valuation assumes $115/ton, adjusted for the high contracted volume in 2026, as discussed earlier. My estimates call for CNR to have a solid 2026 with EBITDA up 125%, but the peak results are likely in 2027. The market values coal stocks at around 6x EV/EBITDA, which is correlated with a mature industry facing slow-motion extinction. The YE26 price target of $129 leaves little room for upside, and while the YE27 price target of $227 is appealing, investor appetite may be volatile, and CNR's shares will move in anticipation of LNG deficits or normalization.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data from a wide range of coal miners worldwide. I assume most forecasts do not yet reflect higher coal prices, but they are starting to trickle in. The sector has been largely forgotten, given the move to cleaner energy, and as demand fell, so have earnings for most companies. The market has, however, begun to price in better results, with price targets in negative territory. While some companies seem to have exceptional growth forecasts for 2026, most are due to a difficult 2025, when oversupply in Indonesia drove many companies into losses. Thus, the growth rates are from depressed levels. Valuations are low, as expected for a mature and declining sector, with most stocks trading under 5.5x EV/EBITDA. On the positive side, the sector has a solid balance sheet with net cash positions.

Risk

There are two key risks to coal price increases: that the war ends soon and that LNG supplies normalize quickly. A secondary risk is regulation, the ability to switch power plants from non-polluting coal to polluting coal. Finally, there is a transport risk: can the interested parties secure bulk ships at reasonable rates to meet delivery demand?

Conclusion

I rate CNR a Hold. Given LNG capacity curtailment and destruction, it is highly likely that power systems dependent on this fuel will switch to coal, which, in turn, should bring prices closer to caloric parity. CNR can generate windfall profits that will drive share valuation and increase capital returns in a more prolonged LNG-deficit scenario, as only 27% of its high-grade coal is expected to capture premium prices in 2026.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Mid Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The S&P 500 Index increased by 2.66% (total return, in USD) in the fourth quarter of 2025, while the Russell 2000 Index rose by 2.21% (total return, in USD). The fourth quarter demonstrated broad resilience, as the major US indices achieved widespread gains despite softening labor market data, a record government shutdown, and increasing scrutiny of heightened artificial intelligence-related expenditures. While initial concerns regarding the sustainability of the artificial intelligence growth theme and elevated valuations led to some volatility and sector rotation, this shift broadened market leadership, further underpinned by robust corporate earnings that indicated fundamental strength. Concurrently, the Federal Open Market Committee continued its path of monetary easing, which further contributed to a broadly positive market outlook. The best performing sectors within the S&P 500 were Health Care, Communication Services, and Financials, while the worst performing sectors were Real Estate, Utilities, and Consumer Staples. For the Russell 2000, the best performing sectors were Health Care, Materials, and Communication Services, while the worst performing sectors were Consumer Staples, Information Technology, and Consumer Discretionary.

Portfolio Attribution

The Goldman Sachs Mid Cap Value Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell Midcap Value Index (net), during the quarter. The Industrials and Energy sectors contributed to returns, while the Materials and Health Care sectors detracted from returns.

Western Digital Corporation (WDC) (1.5%) was a top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. The company is a global provider of solutions for digital content collection, storage, management, protection, and use. Western Digital Corporation's stock price rose throughout the quarter as the company benefitted from continued strong demand within its cloud segment, alongside increasing needs for artificial intelligence-driven data storage. We believe Western Digital Corporation is an industry leader in the hard disk drive market, strategically positioned for structural growth driven by increasing cloud capital expenditure and the emergence of artificial intelligence with its sales increasingly dominated by data centers. This strong market position, coupled with anticipated margin and free cash flow improvements, may offer upside potential.

Please refer to the Fund's prospectus for the most recent expenses.

Our position in Coherent Corp. (COHR) (1.3%) was a top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. The company operates globally in engineered materials, lasers, and photonics. Coherent's stock price rose following its earnings results, delivering better than expected earnings and revenue, robust future guidance, and strong demand for its optical solutions, while demonstrating significant progress towards its margin expansion goals. We believe Coherent presents a compelling investment opportunity due to its market share and capabilities in essential data transmission technology and strong revenue growth across its industrial, telecom, and datacenter segments. The company leverages proprietary laser and optical components, actively expanding its manufacturing to meet high demand for next-generation data solutions. Underpinned by sound financial management, we continue to believe that Coherent will continue to capitalize on long-term trends in artificial intelligence, data centers, and electric vehicles, positioning it well to capture future growth as end-market demand recovers.

Our position in manufacturer and supplier of industrial supplies, tools, safety products, and construction materials, Fastenal Company (FAST) (1.4%) , was a top detractor from relative returns during the quarter. The company narrowly missed consensus expectations in its most recent earnings report, as margins were narrower than anticipated, which led the company's stock to experience pressure. Specifically, the company cited tariff-related cost headwinds and sluggish industrial demand as the key drivers of the margin concerns. However, we believe these concerns are oversold and maintain confidence in the long-term prospects of the company. Specifically, we believe that the company has shown relative sales outperformance and that the operating environment may be at an inflection point, which may benefit top line results. Further, we remain encouraged by the management team's strategic moves, such as investing in improving its inventory mix to better serve customers, which we believe may lay a foundation for future growth. Overall, we continue to maintain confidence in the name as we believe a strong management team, relative sales strength, and an improving demand environment may contribute to a positive outlook.

Our position in International Paper Company (IP) (1.6%) , global supplier of pulp and paper products, was a top detractor from relative returns during the quarter. The company reported mixed results in its most recent quarterly earnings report, with North American revenue exceeding consensus expectations, but international revenues coming in softer due to weakness in European demand. While international demand softness has lingered for longer than originally expected, we believe that we may be at an inflection point for the company. Specifically, as the American containerboard market continues to prioritize the value over the volume of products, we believe that the company's pricing power may remain firm in an array of macroeconomic backdrops. During the quarter, containerboard producing companies began to increase pricing, which we see as a positive readthrough for International Paper Company as this is an indicator that supply reductions in the containerboard vertical are now beginning to positively impact the market.

The company's emphasis on growth and improved efficiency underpins our confidence in the company's management capabilities, which may ultimately lead to long-term outperformance.

Portfolio Review

We initiated a position in Coherent Corp. (COHR) (1.3%) during the quarter. The company operates globally in engineered materials, lasers, and photonics. We believe Coherent presents a compelling investment opportunity due to its market share and capabilities in essential data transmission technology and strong revenue growth across its industrial, telecom, and datacenter segments. The company leverages proprietary laser and optical components, actively expanding its manufacturing to meet high demand for next-generation data solutions. Underpinned by sound financial management, we continue to believe that Coherent will continue to capitalize on long-term trends in artificial intelligence, data centers, and electric vehicles, positioning it well to capture future growth as end-market demand recovers.

We initiated a position in less-than-truckload freight shipping company, Old Dominion Freight Line, Inc. (ODFL) (1.2%) , during the quarter. While the freight shipping industry has experienced some headwinds over the past year, we believe this cycle is at an inflection point and that Old Dominion Freight Line is a compelling name within the industry. Specifically, we believe that the company's yields continue to improve and tons of shipments per day have been stabilizing, making the company an intriguing competitor in the space. Furthermore, we believe that monetary easing, the reduction of tariff noise, and an increase in industrial activity illuminate a more constructive operating environment and a potentially positive path forward.

We exited our position in operator of value-focused membership warehouse clubs, BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (BJ) (0.0%) , during the fourth quarter. While we continue to like and monitor the fundamentals of the company, we believe the operating environment is not currently favorable for the performance of the company. Specifically, amidst pressure on lower/middle income consumers and the company's above-average SNAP exposure, we believe the company is likely to experience softer-than-expected revenue in the near term. Furthermore, the departure of the company's Chief marketing Officer combined with increased promotional competitiveness amongst peers led us to believe the company may experience executional choppiness going into 2026. Overall, while we will continue to monitor the name, we ultimately exited our position to allocate to names we believed had a more favorable growth outlook.

We exited our position in regional bank, Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) (0.0%) , during the fourth quarter. We opportunistically exited the name on recent strength in order to reallocate to other names we believe have a longer runway for growth. Notably, while the company has performed well on the tails of operating environment tailwinds, the management team guided towards increased expense growth in coming quarters, which may pressure margins and lead to muted performance in the medium term. While we continue to like and monitor the name, we ultimately exited our position in favor of more attractive risk/reward opportunities.

Strategy/Outlook

The fourth quarter was defined by improving market breadth, as a solid economic backdrop, monetary easing, and strong corporate earnings results underpinned a positive outlook across sectors. Looking forward, we believe that as evolving trends and technology adoption increasingly drive performance dispersion, active management may be critical in identifying quality names amidst broadening market leadership. Unlike passive management strategies, which may carry exposure to low-quality constituents, taking an active approach may enable investors to avoid potential pitfalls and adapt to shifting macroeconomic conditions and competitive landscape. As we navigate this period of heightened volatility, we remain nimble and look to capitalize on idiosyncratic opportunities uncovered through bottom-up stock selection. We continue to prioritize our quality-oriented approach to investing by focusing on having a long-term viewpoint on the portfolio, seeking businesses with healthy balance sheets, and partnering with management teams that are effective stewards of capital. In our view, we are optimistic that a fundamental approach may generate excess returns in the long run for our clients.

RISK CONSIDERATIONS

The Goldman Sachs Mid Cap Value Fund invests primarily in a diversified portfolio of equity investments in mid-capitalization issuers. The Fund's investments are subject to market risk , which means that the value of the securities in which it invests may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies, particular sectors or governments and/or general economic conditions. The securities of mid- and small-capitalization companies involve greater risks than those associated with larger, more established companies and may be subject to more abrupt or erratic price movements. Foreign and emerging markets investments may be more volatile and less liquid than investments in U.S. securities and are subject to the risks of currency fluctuations and adverse economic, social or political developments. Different investment styles (e.g., "value") tend to shift in and out of favor, and at times the Fund may underperform other funds that invest in similar asset classes. Investing in REITs involves certain unique risks in addition to those risks associated with investing in the real estate industry in general. REITs whose underlying properties are focused in a particular industry or geographic region are also subject to risks affecting such industries and regions. The securities of REITs involve greater risks than those associated with larger, more established companies and may be subject to more abrupt or erratic price movements because of interest rate changes, economic conditions and other factors.

data.title: Palantir Is Becoming The System Behind Decisions
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (PLTR) is no longer just a software company that uses AI. It’s becoming the operating system that governments and companies operate decisions through. The exciting part of this isn’t necessarily the AI but that Palantir sits on top of that, organizing everything into a useful entity.

As AI gets more ubiquitous and less expensive, the interesting part of this equation is that layer that makes it all work. Palantir is already working in defense, growing rapidly in the US commercial space and poised for the next generation of AI, where it’s not simply a helper but a doer.

The argument isn’t whether or not Palantir will grow, but how sustainable that growth trajectory will be. I’m reaffirming my previous strong buy rating for PLTR because of the shift into this type of mission-critical AI infrastructure.

Maven Changes Everything

I believe that the Maven program of record designation is probably one of the most underrated aspects of this entire narrative. The part that catches my eye, though, is that this isn’t so much about Anthropic as it is about who the Pentagon actually trusts. When the DoD blacklists Anthropic because of usage concerns, what they’re really saying is that alignment, control, and governance are more important than the capabilities of the models. This is where Palantir benefits.

From my perspective, this is simply another affirmation of the thesis that Palantir is the orchestration layer that governments need when the stakes are high. While the pullback in the stock and the valuation concerns are legitimate, in my opinion, this is simply another tailwind. Defense AI is becoming more consolidated around who the Pentagon trusts, and Palantir is becoming the center of that consolidation.

The Metric Everyone Missed

What I find most interesting about Palantir is not the underlying growth rate, which is significant and should be acknowledged, but the underlying structure in which that growth is represented. Palantir reported $4.3 billion in total contract value in one single quarter, and their top 20 customers spend roughly $94 million each year, growing 45% year over year.

Normally, that would be cause for concern, but in the case of Palantir, I see something entirely different. They are not growing by expanding their number of customers; they are expanding their reach into their existing customers. The 139% net dollar growth is interesting but not as interesting as the underlying reality that revenue is growing more quickly than their number of customers, and their underlying margins are incredibly high, even in excess of 50% when measured against free cash flow.

Most software businesses have to make a choice between growth and profitability. Palantir is managing to do both simultaneously. To my mind, that is not indicative of the traditional SaaS business but more indicative of a decision-layer monopoly in institutions. If that is the case, then traditional measures of growth, such as seat counts or revenue growth, do not quite do justice to the larger picture. The real rate of growth is in terms of institutional dependency and that is clearly accelerating far more quickly than people in the market recognize.

Palantir’s Agentic AI Inflection Point

I think the most interesting and in many ways, least predictable piece of the narrative is the shift towards agentic AI. That is where the narrative shifts from tools to control systems. With generative AI, you can put it in front of other workflows and not change the underlying nature of the institution itself. Agentic AI is different because it’s systems that can take actions, make decisions given certain constraints and interact with different workflows where mistakes have real-world consequences. So that’s where Palantir’s ontology is so important.

It’s the ontology that is creating the structure and the governance to allow these AI systems to function in these complex institutions. Without that level of ontology, it’s very difficult to trust these systems and it’s even more difficult to scale them in these institutions. Customer behavior is already beginning to show the inflection point in the market. Net dollar retention is still very strong at 139%, and our existing customers are not only expanding in terms of dollars but also in terms of the number of use cases in which our platform is being used. The platform is moving deeper into the institutions, not just sitting on the periphery.

I also suspect that the AIP marketplace, while still early, is a very interesting concept. If Palantir can deliver a reusable agent that can be distributed across companies, then this business model could start to look like a platform with network effects. I wouldn’t necessarily build that into the model but I think there are network effects that are not necessarily being factored into this market.

This Valuation Prices a Future as Infrastructure

What I think is most interesting in the way that this valuation works out is not necessarily the growth, but how that growth progresses. The general consensus for how this growth progresses in the future is that revenue will be around $7.3 billion in 2026 and then up to $10 billion in 2027. This means that there will be a significant deceleration in the way that the P/S ratios compress from around 47x to the low 30s.

Part of this guidance does make sense. The way that this guidance works out, the revenue for 2026 will be around $7.19 billion, along with more than $4 billion in adjusted operating income and nearly $4 billion in free cash flow. That’s a very solid performance by any standard. What I believe is most important, however, is how sustainable this growth is. If Palantir can maintain a free cash flow margin of even 35% to 40%, then this means that if revenue is $10 billion, free cash flow would be $3.5 to $4 billion. When you think about this, this starts to look a lot different.

Finally, what makes this investment case unique is that even if you apply the Rule of 40, this number is already well above 100. I think that this number will normalize but even if that’s the case, this still looks a lot like infrastructure. This is what the market is effectively pricing. Not only growth but also the possibility of Palantir becoming a foundational layer in critical systems.

The Real Risk Is a Broken Trajectory

The real risk, from my perspective, is not a correction in the multiple, nor is it a compression of the multiple. These are surface-level issues. The real issue is whether the trajectory holds.

If commercial growth in the US slows significantly, or if it was front-end loaded, this challenges an important assumption in the story. If government revenue continues to grow well, but becomes unbalanced in favor of a few programs, this creates a new type of risk. Finally, if international growth continues to disappoint, with no signs of meaningful improvement, this calls into question the optionality in the story.

I am also watching for scalability. The Palantir model relies on forward-deployed engineers to drive growth. This model has been incredibly successful to date, but it is unclear whether this model can be sustained in perpetuity without some form of constraint. This is not an immediate concern, but it is a signal that I suspect is far more meaningful than any of the quarterly data points.

Bottom Line

Palantir is a company that, in my view, is well beyond the typical AI story, into something much, much deeper in the structure of the market. The opportunity is real, but so are the expectations. I remain very bullish on the stock but I would look for continued signals of deeper entrenchment within the market.

data.title: S&P 500 Continues Falling As Fed's Crystal Ball Gets Foggy
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) has dropped for four consecutive weeks, coinciding with the market-moving geopolitical event of the Iran war that began on 28 February 2026. In the fourth week since the Iran war began, the index dropped 2.1% to close out the week at 6,368.85.

Through these four weeks, the S&P 500 has been the strongest of the three major U.S. stock market indices in losing 7.4% of its value since the close of trading on 27 February 2026. The S&P 500 is the only one that hasn't yet confirmed a correction, defined as a 10% decline from its last peak. The index last peaked at 6,978.59 on 27 January 2026 and is now a little over 8.7% below it.

The market-moving headlines during the fourth week of March 2026 highlighted the problems Federal Reserve officials are having in reading the state of the U.S. economy to determine the path they will take in setting U.S. interest rates. For its part, the CME Group's FedWatch Tool cut through the Fed's foggy analytical clutter to anticipate no interest rate changes through the end of 2026. Instead, the tool indicates a low probability of rate hikes, giving about a 3 out of 10 chance of a quarter-point rate hike in the Federal Funds Rate being announced after the Fed's Open Market Committee meets on 28 October (2026-Q4).

The latest update of the alternative futures chart shows the S&P 500 continued a downward trajectory below the redzone forecast range we added to the chart at the end of February 2026. The centerline of that forecast range is effectively functioning as a counterfactual, indicating where the trajectory the S&P 500 would have taken in the absence of the ongoing geopolitical event.

Through the close of trading on 27 March 2026, the S&P 500 is about 7.3% below that level.
