data.title: Northrop Grumman: Undervalued Ahead Of Key Program Ramp
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Northrop Grumman (NOC) is looking to benefit from both tailwinds and multiple long-duration programs as it recovers from a period of margin compression. The B-21 Raider transition into full-scale production is one of the most important catalysts, where a margin headwind turns into a multi-year revenue and earnings driver. With the recent production increase not reflected in guidance, there will likely be upward revisions.

Although there has been some near-term pressure from the Sentinel program, it appears priced in, and cancellation of the project is unlikely due to necessity. Northrop is also moving into autonomous aircraft, with contracts from both the Marines and Air Force. This makes them one of the first in collaborative combat aircraft, which has the potential to match or surpass the value of the current fighter programs over time.

Defense spending is higher than ever, and with market share in multiple defense segments, Northrop should benefit. This is why I'm rating the stock a Buy, with a 12-month price target of $754.75 per share.

B-21 Raider

The B-21 Raider is the replacement for the B-2 Spirit as the US Air Force's stealth bomber. A contract worth $4.5 billion was recently signed, which will increase production of the bomber by 25%. This contract was not included in the company's guidance, which leaves room for upward revisions as the year goes on. As B-21 production accelerates, revenue and margin benefits should begin showing.

The program has had its challenges with a $477 million loss provision related to low-rate initial production (LRIP), ultimately putting a drag on margins. These should recover as the company moves past the LRIP phase and increases production.

Sentinel ICBM

The Sentinel ICBM program has made trouble for Northrop, exceeding both cost and timeline targets. It was, at one point, 81% over budget and three years late. While the program is still continuing, it has been restructured, with Congress giving it $2.5 billion for risk reduction. This is a concern among some investors, but the market is well aware of this. Since the US relies on much older 1970s ICBMs and Congress has approved funding, it's unlikely the program will be cancelled. With that in mind, the only concern is how much the program will hurt margins as it continues.

Defense Spending Tailwinds

Global defense spending has continued to increase and is expected to continue doing so in the coming years, meaning the entire sector is now in a supercycle. This is supported by projections of over $2.6 trillion in spending for global defense in 2026, and the US becoming the first trillion-dollar defense spender. This isn't projected to be a one-year spike with a rising number of global conflicts, creating sustained demand for defense. With Northrop offering bombers, ICBMs, space systems, and autonomous aircraft, the company is well-positioned to take advantage of this supercycle.

Autonomous Systems

With autonomous warfare becoming more common, autonomous aircraft are a segment that could be a major revenue driver over the next decade. In January, the US Marines selected Northrop and Kratos to make its first CCA, which are AI-powered drones designed to fly next to manned fighters. This contract is worth $231.5 million and turns the Kratos XQ-58 Valkyrie from testing into operation through Northrop. This is also the first CCA program to move into development for the Marines.

The Air Force is also looking at Northrop's Project Talon for its own CCA program, with potential contracts expected in 2026. This plan calls for hundreds of CCAs by the end of the decade, which creates a TAM that could eventually surpass traditional fighter programs in scale.

Northrop has autonomous experience with its RQ-4B Global Hawk and MQ-4C Triton as high-altitude long-endurance (HALE) drones. They have other milestones, such as the first unmanned aircraft to land on an aircraft carrier (X-47B) and a program developing vertical takeoff/landing for autonomous aircraft on ships. This opportunity represents a whole new category of revenue, not a replacement. If they can continue to win contracts, which looks likely, it could be a major revenue pillar as the US continues to turn toward autonomous technology.

Valuation

Northrop trades slightly above peers like Lockheed Martin (~21x non-GAAP forward P/E) and General Dynamics (~22x non-GAAP forward P/E) with a forward non-GAAP P/E of ~25x. This small premium is justifiable, however, with the B-21 production ramp creating multi-year revenue and margin upside and the autonomous aircraft potential.

Using 2027 expected EPS of $30.19 and a 25x P/E multiple, my 12-month price target is $754.75 per share, representing upside of ~11% (~12% with dividend). I believe the current valuation underprices the upcoming production ramp, continued tailwinds, and the expected autonomous aircraft contracts.

Risks

The defense budget is the largest risk, with the potential for growth to miss projections. Although this seems unlikely given growing conflicts, it is a possibility.

The Sentinel program is another risk, with the potential for margin pressure if it continues to pile up costs and miss project goals. This program has been restructured, but the risk is still there.

Conclusion

Northrop Grumman is looking to take advantage of a defense supercycle after facing recent margin pressures. The company has strong long-term growth potential with market share in multiple defense segments, B-21 production increases, and autonomous aircraft contracts. These points suggest that Northrop's current share price is undervalued. This is why I'm rating the company a Buy, with a price target of $754.75 per share.

data.title: Kamada: From Buy To Hold After 22.4% Surge (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since my article, "My Top 5 Biotech Stocks Big Pharma Could Buy Next," Kamada shares (KMDA) have risen 22.4%, outperforming the so-called "bellwether" for the biotech investors, the iShares Biotechnology ETF [6.5% return] (IBB), as well as the S&P 500 [-3.9%] (SPY).

And to my surprise, this happened even after it announced on December 8 last year that it was discontinuing its Phase 3 InnovAATe study [NCT04204252], because Inhaled AAT was unlikely to improve lung function measured by FEV1.

On the other hand, KMDA's revenue rose 11.8% year-over-year to $44.12 million, beating my "base case" scenario by $1.6 million.

And its non-GAAP EPS came below the consensus estimate, as it had in 2 of the previous 7 quarters, but this time by 2 cents.

Now, I will start to answer whether, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, Kamada still has an appealing risk/reward ratio or whether it's time to downgrade its rating from Buy to Hold or even to Sell.

Kamada is capturing the plasma market

So, I will continue with Kamada's Proprietary Products segment, which is quite rich and includes 8 medications.

Because it's one of the leaders in the plasma-derived medicinal products market, the revenue of this unit grew 21.7% year-on-year to $38.2 million in Q4.

And to be more specific, in my view, the two key drugs are KamRAB/KedRAB and Glassia.

The first one is HRIG, approved by the FDA, among other regulatory authorities, for transient post-exposure prophylaxis.

By the way, KedRab's mechanism of action is quite simple.

After injection at the site of the bite, neutralizing antibodies bind to the rabies virus and, as a result, prevent progressive encephalomyelitis.

And according to PubMed, if this neurological condition is left untreated, it causes over 99% of fatal cases.

Overall, when I opened the CDC, I discovered that about 4,000 rabies cases are registered annually in the U.S. alone.

And yet, 100,000 Americans receive post-exposure prophylaxis.

This, in my estimation, is one of the reasons why KamRAB/KedRAB generated $70.8 million in revenue for Kamada in 2025, up 123.3% YoY.

And, during the Q4 earnings call, CEO Amir London was quite enthusiastic about KedRAB/KamRAB's prospects.

Our lead product continues to be our anti-rabies immunoglobulin, KEDRAB, which is being distributed in the U.S. through our collaboration with Kedrion. Sales of the product to Kedrion increased in 2025 to approximately $54 million, well above the contract minimum commitment.

We have a firm commitment of $90 million from Kedrion for minimum orders from 2026 through 2027 and our current supply agreement with them runs through 2031.

In addition to our significant market share in the U.S. we continue to grow sales of KAMRAB in leading international markets such as Canada, Latin American countries and Israel.

Glassia, however, is approved for the treatment of an autosomal recessive disorder called AATD [alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency], which, as you might understand, is caused by low levels of AAT in the plasma and tissues.

Ultimately, this dramatically increases the risk of lung and liver disease.

According to Orphanet, the prevalence of this condition ranges from 1/1,600 to 1/5,000 in Western Europe and the U.S.

In 2025, Glassia net revenues were $35 million, beating my "best case" scenario by $2.1 million, despite competition from CSL's Zemaira (CSLLY) and Grifols' Prolastin-C (GRFS).

I believe one of the factors for the increased demand is the FDA's approval of it in 4 and 5-gram single-dose vials.

Previously, only 1-gram vials were available. This means patients with AATD experienced significant discomfort due to more than 4 infusions at a time.

And as KMDA's partner Takeda (TAK) announced on March 11 last year, new vial sizes of Glassia will be available starting in July 2025.

But that's, of course, not all I wanted to talk about.

On March 26, the FDA approved Kamada's San Antonio-based plasma collection center.

It has a capacity of about 50,000 liters per year at full capacity and will generate annual revenues of $8 million to $10 million.

Much to my regret, Kamada doesn't note sales of plasma collected in the other sites in its 6-K/20-F reports.

Risks

Now, it's time for my table with risks.

The key ones are the ongoing war in the Middle East, which has already driven up hydrocarbon prices, RFK Jr./Trump's information pressure on the pharma industry.

Also, Kamada will receive royalty payments from Takeda at a rate of 6% from August 2025 to 2040, the previous 12%. As Kamada is currently making money ($25.5M in 2025), it does not have cash burn or a cash runway and has been increasing its working capital ($75.5M at the end of 2025).

Takeaway

I didn't mention earlier several factors that keep me from downgrading Kamada's rating to "Sell."

So, its non-GAAP EPS will grow at double/single-digit percentages [from 4.7% to 44.3%] and reach $0.78 in 2030. That is, KMDA's P/E ratio will drop from its current 23.3x to 10.52x, which, in my opinion, indicates that it is trading at a discount to its rivals, Takeda and CSL.

And secondly, according to SA's data, the average PT for Kamada is $13, which implies an upside of about 59.5% from its current stock price.

However, due to the risks I mentioned earlier, and the fact that Kamada manufactured its plasma-derived medicines in Beit Kama, about 25.8 km from Be'er Sheva, I believe its risk/reward profile has become more balanced.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Global Economy Faces Crossroads As Pentagon Preps Ground Assault
data.description:
Background

The Washington Post has reported that the Pentagon is preparing for substantial ground operations in Iran. This follows on the heels of reporting from Axios, suggesting that the Pentagon was preparing options for a final blow. As of now, the rumored plans call for targeted and limited operations rather than a full-scale invasion. At the very least, however, any substantial ground operations are a big step on what could be a very slippery slope.

Most importantly, for this discussion, what happens in the region could have a major impact on energy and trade, thus in turn impacting the global economy and markets worldwide. Food security and agriculture, too, could evolve into a serious concern as fertilizer shipments are blocked. Below, we see that once the conflict kicked off on February 28th, the S&P 500 went into a rather dramatic decline. Slippery slope indeed.

Markets and the global economy have already been dramatically impacted by the air war, but a ground war will likely exacerbate many of the economic pressures. Even before recent developments, recession odds had already been climbing. Some experts suggest that the global economy could slump at the two-month mark, which is fast approaching, assuming the Strait remains closed. My gut says we're further away from a recession, but the risks are compounding no matter how you cut it.

Against this backdrop, investors should be on their toes. Below, I outline several potential developments and what I believe could unfold. For investors, anticipating various scenarios could speed up reaction times. Of course, individual investors can and should incorporate their personal outlook and insights while considering the outcome of various scenarios. I will also highlight what I believe are some of the more likely outcomes, but I suggest investors keep a tab on developments and adjust as needed.

Outlook Considerations and Thesis

Many Asian stock markets started this week in the red, as shown above. Yet futures for American indices were posting minor gains ahead of the market opening, perhaps driven upward by claims that Iran was ready to accept many of the conditions put forward by the American government. Overall, markets will likely experience turbulence this week. While saber-rattling or likewise hints of peace could impact markets, I would expect these swings to be temporary. Markets might continue to slope downward, but I personally will not be inclined to pull out of the market or take dramatic defensive steps. Meanwhile, prospects of peace would be more likely to cool energy prices while bolstering stock markets.

If a major ground incursion does kick off, however, I expect major market disruptions with energy prices skyrocketing and stock markets slumping. The initial impact will likely be dramatic. Still, if the United States were to quickly deliver a final blow to the Iranian regime, as the Pentagon intends, thus ending the war, markets would likely surge while oil and energy prices would suffer a more moderate (but still substantial) decline.

Whatever unfolds, so long as the Strait of Hormuz remains impassable, we will inch closer and closer to a global economic contraction. What do I think will unfold? So far, the Iranian government publicly remains uncompromising, and its recently released maximalist demands (as explored later) will only add fuel to the fire. The American government, meanwhile, has already proven that it's willing to exert intense force on Iran. Ultimately, I believe the odds of an incursion will increase dramatically if there are no breakthroughs in negotiations before the 10-day pause on striking Iran's oil infrastructure expires.

For now, I'm assigning a hold rating for markets in general. My own portfolio leans defensive, owing in part to my general concerns about the economy over the past year, so I am not compelled to make drastic moves right now. Many of my picks strike me as attractive regardless of economic concerns. If I weren't already on somewhat defensive footing, I likely would make some adjustments. Investors need to curate their own portfolios, of course, but I recommend examining the risk profile in light of the potential impact of a ground invasion and the continued disruptions of the global oil and gas market.

The Impact On Markets Past, Present, And Future

So far, the war with Iran has largely suppressed stock markets. Granted, last week saw some strong short-term bumps pushing against a general slump (shown below), while oil prices cooled (shown above) based on reports that Iran and the United States might be able to cut a deal. Further signals suggesting peace will likely result in favorable market bumps. Of course, actually getting ink to paper and words into action is no small task, as I’ve discussed elsewhere.

Iran has effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz, using combinations of drones, missiles, and fast boats to threaten ships and keep shipments bottled up on the wrong side of the Strait of Hormuz. Importantly, this asymmetric approach could prove difficult for the American military to counter, which in turn could cause further market instability. Energy prices have been climbing, threatening a global economy that has already shown a variety of weaknesses. Oil and gas infrastructure across the region has been destroyed and may restrain oil production for years to come.

So far, reports indicate the Pentagon is focused on more limited ground incursions, but boots would still be on the ground and exposed to dangers, including drones. Any major developments or headlines could end up reflected in market movements. If significant ground operations are launched, I believe the short-term pattern with markets will mirror what we saw with the launch of the air campaign. First, markets would be likely to sink on the prospects of regional instability, energy disruptions, and escalating attacks. Below, we see how the SP500 reacted to the initial air attacks. I'd expect a similar market retreat if boots hit the ground.

Owing to superior technology and manpower, I would expect the American military to secure early victories, which would likely bolster markets. The prospects of a short war, and in this round, the prospects of opening up the Strait of Hormuz could bolster markets. We saw oil prices decline at the start of last week based on positive indications of peace from the White House. However, responding statements from the Iranian government caused oil prices to spike. This turbulence is illustrated below. I would expect similar turbulence in the event of a ground war. Developments that look positive for the United States will likely bolster markets, while negative headlines will weigh them down.

In the face of air strikes, Iran has thus far used asymmetrical attacks to shut down the Strait of Hormuz, which is why I believe we've seen a sustained downward trend in the market. In a ground war, asymmetrical attacks may become even more dangerous and thus could cause an even steeper decline.

Perhaps the most important question is whether or not the United States can get shipments moving again. If so, markets should react bullishly. For now, I am at hold, waiting to see not only if the war will escalate, but also if the United States can secure victories and open up the Strait. If this does happen, I would expect a substantial stock market boost. Meanwhile, I believe much of the prior and near-term risks of the conflict have been priced in over prior weeks, which is why I am not at a sell rating. If medium- and long-term risks increase, however, my outlook could quickly be downgraded.

The Global Economy Is Already Balancing On A Tight Rope

There’s been a lot of coverage on the global economy, recession risks, etc. Here, I'm focused on developments tied closely to a potential ground war with Iran. However, that doesn’t mean we should ignore other economic risks.

Among other things, a protracted war with Iran could strain the global economy just enough that it causes other conditions to deteriorate. For example, interest rates might spike based on the American government borrowing to pay for the war. This, in turn, might contribute to, say, a corporate credit crisis, or housing crisis, or whatever.

Overall, energy prices have risen considerably since the start of the conflict, as shown with the WTI crude chart above. The Iranian military has struck energy infrastructure across the region. Claims are circulating that suggest up to 40% of the oil and gas infrastructure in the region has been impacted. Should the war escalate, I would expect more energy infrastructure to be targeted.

Sustained high energy prices will also drain consumer spending the world over. This is especially serious because the global economy is driven by consumer consumption. Consumer outlook has already been on the decline (as illustrated below), and the war plus high energy prices will likely worsen the mood. The American economy (and to some extent, the global economy) has been sustained by AI investing, but surging energy prices could cool spending on that front, given how energy-intensive AI is.

While I am at a hold for now, believing that near-term risks are largely priced in, I do believe there are considerable medium-term risks. For example, consumers may shrug off rising fuel prices over the next few weeks, but by the time the summer rolls around and air conditioners are being turned on (increasing energy demand), we may see more dramatic adjustments in consumer behavior. These medium-term risks could dramatically gain momentum, and thus, if we don't see progress towards opening up the Strait and securing peace, or at least a cease-fire, my outlook could quickly sour.

Defensive Positioning Considerations

Developing a defensive strategy can take a fair bit of time unless you're looking to adopt the traditional tactics. I'm not ready to outline a full defensive strategy for this situation, but I do want to mention a few considerations:

  • Low supply typically means high prices (all other things being equal). With fertilizers taking a hit, decreasing agricultural output, coupled with steady demand, could result in rising food costs, thus boosting revenues for agriculture companies.
  • Wars are costly, and America's public debt is already becoming a worry. Inflation will also likely surge if the war drags on. These factors, among others, make treasuries less attractive right now than is typical.
  • While I expect AI to take a hit, some companies like Alphabet and Amazon strike me as well-positioned to weather the storm.
  • The shutdown of the Strait suggests that alternative energy sources should be considered a national security concern. Companies and governments may increase spending on alternative energy.

I'm hoping to dive into the above considerations at some point, and I may end up revamping my views while doing so. For now, I simply wanted to provide some food for thought.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Markets have proven quite resilient through thick and thin in recent months. As the war in Iran drags on, markets may build "immunity" to geopolitical developments. In this scenario, developments, whether towards peace or increasing tensions, may become muted. Further, as public policymakers and business leaders become more familiar with the current conflict-impacted environment, they may devise strategies that mute turbulence to some extent.

Key Take-Away: Buckle Up, But Don’t Panic, and Keep Your Eyes On The Horizon

If the conflict escalates, I expect market turmoil. I can stomach the immediate risks; hence, my hold rating. That said, my outlook is influenced by my prior defensive lean. If I were exposed, I'd be more inclined to make adjustments right now. Regardless, what’s arguably most important is the long-term health of the economy. I believe the global economy is in some ways weaker than it appears, but at the same time, it has proven resilient. A prolonged war in Iran, especially if more regional energy production is hit, could be enough to break the global economy’s back.

For investors, I wouldn’t panic at reactive market bumps like we saw last week. Some of the turmoil should prove temporary, for now. That said, the real and serious risk is the risk of a global economic contraction. A ground war with Iran, on its own, won’t make that outcome inevitable, but could it be the straw that breaks the back? Unfortunately, I do believe medium-term risks are mounting, and investors need to stay tuned in. If peace or at least a détente is found, I believe the global economy will chug on. If not? My outlook will likely sour quickly, and I may start to move into a more defensive position.

data.title: Stock Market Breadth: Warning Or Opportunity?
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) is down roughly 7% from its January 27 all-time high. Unsurprisingly, the media is full of “red” headlines discussing the seemingly “endless” correction we are in. Unsurprisingly, previously complacent investors are now anxious, as nothing seems to be working. But that index-level headline conceals something far more alarming: stock market breadth has collapsed. According to Morgan Stanley, approximately 42% of S&P 500 members are already down 20% or more from their 52-week highs. More than 200 companies are in their own private bear markets, even as the index itself is not.

This was a point we noted in this past weekend’s Bull Bear Report:

“J.P. Morgan captured the paradox: the S&P 500 is down only ~9% despite oil rising 70% and the Fed shifting from pricing two cuts to a 50% probability of a hike, and software falling 20%. As we noted recently, a much larger correction is underway in the market.”

While “times have been tough lately,” this is not a new phenomenon. Stock market breadth deterioration almost always precedes index-level damage, not the other way around. On March 9th, we noted in Technical Deterioration: Risk Management Is Key:

“More importantly, the RSI exhibited a textbook bearish divergence at the all-time high: price made a new peak, but momentum did not confirm. We repeatedly discussed that divergence was the earliest signal of the distribution phase now unfolding. With the RSI not in oversold territory below 30, there is room for more pressure before a technical bounce becomes probable.”

Here is an updated chart showing that previous divergence. Along with waning stock market breadth, relative strength is now in oversold territory.

What’s unusual today is the degree of divergence between individual stocks and the cap-weighted index. When a handful of stocks carry enough weight to paper over widespread internal damage, investors holding diversified portfolios feel the pain long before the headlines acknowledge it.

Furthermore, as detailed in The 200-DMA Just Broke, the deterioration is not uncommon of corrective markets. That break, combined with deeply oversold momentum readings and AAII bearish sentiment, creates a historically specific setup.

The breadth story is quite fascinating. The software sector has 97% of its S&P 500 members 20% or more below their respective 52-week highs. Automobile stocks follow at 75%, with media and entertainment at 63%.

The other end of the distribution is equally instructive. Energy stocks have zero members in bear territory. Utilities sit at just 6%, and consumer staples at 14%. Those numbers confirm the rotation that’s been underway since January.

“Significant rotation trades, characterized by heavy trading activity in and out of various sectors and factors, have led to large daily divergences in the performance of certain sectors. The market’s surface may look calm, but beneath it, passive investors are actively shifting between narratives, valuations, and risk exposures.” — RIA Advisors, February 2026

So, what likely happens next?

Reading the 200-DMA Break: Six Signals, Mixed Picture

Since 2000, the S&P 500 has broken its 200-DMA on a sustained basis seven times. The average one-month return following those breaks was -5.3%, and none produced a positive first-month return. The average 12-month return after a sustained break was -4.0%.

But the distinction between a sustained break and a reflexive whipsaw matters enormously. When the 200-DMA was already flat or declining before the price crossed below, every major bear market since 2000 followed: 2000, 2008, 2022. When the 200-DMA was still rising at the break, as it is currently, the average 12-month return was +19.8%, with a 100% hit rate for positive returns at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months.

Today’s scorecard is mixed. The 200-DMA is still rising, RSI is now below 30, and AAII bearish sentiment has risen sharply and well above the 45% contrarian threshold. These are all still bullish. Against that, the weekly MACD had already turned negative before the price break, which has preceded every sustained bear since 2000. Stock market breadth, measured by the percentage of S&P 500 stocks above their 200-day moving averages, has dropped sharply and is below the 60% level that historically characterizes whipsaw recoveries.

Bank of America’s Michael Hartnett described the current environment as approaching a “buyable washout.” He is probably correct, but until the Iran situation is resolved, or at least a path to resolution is visible, the risk of a deeper decline cannot be discounted. However, investors shouldn’t panic-sell this correction. As JPMorgan’s global market strategist, Jack Manley, noted:

“When there’s a bad sell-off, that bad sell-off is typically followed by a strong bounce back. Given the nature of this sell-off, the likelihood for that bounce back, whenever it occurs, to be pretty concentrated and pretty powerful is that much higher.”

In that previous article, we examined every instance since 2000 where all three conditions aligned simultaneously: stock market breadth deterioration with 40% or more of S&P 500 members in bear territory, the index trading below its 200-DMA, and both MACD and RSI in oversold territory. Six comparable episodes emerged:

  • October 2002,
  • March 2009,
  • February 2016,
  • December 2018,
  • March 2020, and
  • October 2022.

If those dates don’t mean anything to you, those were the months that previous corrections and bear markets ended…not began.

Yes, the near-term picture is uncomfortable, and the average one-month return in these setups is -2.1%, with only 42% of periods producing positive outcomes. While pain tends to extend, and a lower low within one to three months is the historical norm rather than the exception, it serves to wash out weaker hands.

It is the medium- and long-term data that convey the real message. By 12 months, the average return climbs to +14.6%, with 75% of comparable periods producing positive outcomes. At 24 months, average returns reach +26.3%, and the positive hit rate rises to 83%. The investor who stayed positioned through the fear, in all six of these episodes, came out far ahead of the investor who sold into it.

The Permanent Cost of Panic Selling

The single most damaging decision most investors make during periods of falling stock market breadth is selling. The data on this is unambiguous. Seven of the market’s 10 best days in any given 20-year period occur within two weeks of the 10 worst days, according to JPMorgan Asset Management research. The best days follow the worst days because fear-driven selling creates dislocations that are rapidly corrected. You can see this in the chart below that the best and worst days are clustered together.

In other words, while investors are always told to just “buy and hold” because they will miss the 10 best days if they don’t, investors should focus on mitigating the risk of significant capital losses during those periods.

This doesn’t mean you can effectively miss all the bad days; however, given that higher-volatility periods tend to cluster, understanding when to reduce exposure can significantly improve outcomes over time. Even if you miss the 10 best days along the way. That math applies with particular force in setups like the current one. Since 1974, according to data compiled by Clear Perspective Advisors, the S&P 500 has returned more than 24% on average following a market correction. Only 25% of the 48 corrections since World War II have progressed into full bear markets. In other words, there is a 75% chance this correction will not turn into a bear market. However, dismissing that 25% entirely is just as foolish for future outcomes.

The One Variable That Changes Everything

The honest caveat to all this data is the recession. The two worst outcomes in the six-episode dataset, 2002 and 2008, were both accompanied by genuine economic contractions that extended the drawdown far beyond what the averages suggest. In those cases, forward returns at 12 months were still negative.

Today’s macro environment doesn’t yet show the classic recessionary signatures that preceded those two episodes. The 200-DMA is still rising, not declining, but time will change that. The Fed retains room to cut rates, but higher sustained oil prices could curtail that. Furthermore, deeply oversold sentiment indicators have historically correlated more with fear peaks than with the beginning of prolonged selling cycles. But oversold markets can, and have, become even more so previously.

Whether the Iran conflict and its oil price transmission into consumer spending and corporate margins eventually tip the economy into contraction remains the central unresolved question. That question is the one thing that investors need to guard against the most.

Goldman Sachs has held its 7,600 year-end S&P 500 target through the recent sell-off, anchored by projections of roughly $309 per share in 2026 earnings and $342 in 2027. That base case rests on 12% earnings growth and an economy that continues to expand despite the headwinds from the Iran conflict. Goldman’s own bear cases are sobering: a moderate growth shock takes the index to 6,300, while a severe oil-driven disruption could push it as low as 5,400, with the forward P/E compressing from 21x to 16x in the worst case.

JPMorgan has moved more decisively in the other direction, cutting its year-end target to 7,200 from 7,500 on March 19, citing oil supply shut-ins of 8 million barrels per day, the highest on record, and warning that markets are dangerously underestimating the demand destruction risk. JPMorgan strategist Dubravko Lakos-Bujas explicitly flagged near-term downside to 6,000–6,200, noting that four of five oil shocks since the 1970s have preceded a recession. Neither bank is calling for 2008. But the spread between their base cases and their downside scenarios has rarely been this wide.

Here is the most important point.

“Stock market breadth will eventually resolve, either by individual stocks recovering toward the benchmark level, or by the index itself catching down to the damage that’s already been inflicted.”

History says the former is far more likely given the current configuration of indicators. Given that backdrop, here are some steps to consider with respect to your own personal situation, goals, and objectives.

Stock market breadth, by any measure, is at levels historically associated with significant forward returns for patient investors. Three of the six key indicators that separate a brief, recoverable 200-DMA break from a sustained bear market are currently bullish, not bearish. That doesn’t mean the pain is over, as near-term data suggest a lower low is possible. However, for investors who can navigate the storm, clearer skies and calmer tides will eventually prevail.

The goal isn’t to time the bottom. Nobody does that consistently. The goal is to avoid permanent capital impairment from panic selling, reduce risk through disciplined rebalancing, and be positioned to participate in the recovery. Based on every comparable episode in the modern era, that recovery has come, and it has come faster than the fear of the moment would suggest.

SOURCES & METHODOLOGY

Forward return data: RIA Advisors analysis of six comparable historical episodes (Oct 2002, Mar 2009, Feb 2016, Dec 2018, Mar 2020, Oct 2022) where all three conditions — 40%+ of S&P 500 stocks in bear territory, index below 200-DMA, and RSI/MACD oversold — were simultaneously present. Sector breadth data: Morgan Stanley, via RIA Advisors, “The Bear Market Hiding Beneath the Calm Surface” (March 2026). 200-DMA break analysis: RIA Advisors, “The 200-DMA Just Broke: What Every Investor Should Know” (March 23, 2026). JPMorgan best-days data: CNBC, April 2025, citing JPMorgan Asset Management. AAII sentiment: aaii.com. Fear & Greed Index: feargreedmeter.com. Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan price target data: TheStreet, March 2026. Corrections history: Carson Wealth, Ned Davis Research, Hartford Funds.

This article is for informational and educational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Investing involves risk, including the potential loss of principal.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla: Promises Of Robots And Space Data Centers Do Not Justify This Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) today stands at a $1.4 trillion market cap, trading at an extraordinarily high 179x forward P/E ratio. In my previous article on TSLA, I covered how TSLA’s insistence on vision-only self-driving technology is not going to be competitive with other robotaxi companies like Alphabet Inc.’s (GOOG) Waymo, which are already steadily expanding globally with their technology based on lidar, radar, cameras, and HD maps, while TSLA’s robotaxis struggle to get regulatory approval. I also discussed how TSLA’s first-mover advantage in the EV market has eroded due to competitor EVs catching up.

One of the core critiques of my article was that robotaxis are only one of many drivers of growth for TSLA. These drivers of growth are Optimus robots, energy systems, and the new focus on semiconductors, with the recently announced Austin chip factory. I am not convinced by any of these drivers of growth, and I maintain my previous rating of Strong Sell.

Since writing my first article on TSLA, shares are down almost 25%, and I believe there is still room for further decline. I believe that the energy segment is the sole bright spot in TSLA’s business, Optimus robots are far from mass production and currently not even competitive relative to other players, and the new semiconductor fabs are going to burn capital and dilute shareholder value with little likelihood for competitiveness.

The Energy Business Is Actually Quite Good

The sole area of consistent fundamental strength in the TSLA portfolio is the energy generation and storage division. Since 2021, the energy division has shown growth of +357%, far outpacing the growth of any other TSLA division. To put this in context, the energy division showed a 5-year CAGR of 46.28%, while the automotive division showed 10.15% and the services & other revenue segment showed 34.74%.

Not only is the top line of this division growing, but the energy segment is profitable as well. The profitability of this segment acts as a sort of offset to the contracting margins seen in the automotive division. The energy storage business showed a gross margin of 28.7% in Q4 2025, with quarterly gross profit reaching an all-time high of $1.1 billion.

TSLA has very well capitalized on the market backdrop behind the energy business. As renewable generation expands, the grid needs more storage. Products such as Megapack are designed for repeatable deployment. This allows TSLA to sell into large utility and commercial projects rather than relying solely on fragmented consumer demand. Additionally, TSLA’s vertical integration provides a significant cost and supply advantage over competitors like Fluence Energy, Inc. (FLNC).

I expect growth within this division to continue, and I view this part of the business as the sole bright spot within TSLA. Supporting my view of continued growth within the energy division are significant expansions to manufacturing capacity: the Shanghai Megafactory began operations in February 2025, and the Houston plant is expected to begin operations by the end of 2026.

The Supposed Robotics Revolution

The largest component of the “TSLA’s valuation is forward-looking” argument is the Optimus humanoid robot. Tesla CEO Elon Musk, during the Q4 earnings call, boldly said,

We're going to take the Model S and X production space in our Fremont factory and convert that into an Optimus factory, which will -- with the long-term goal of having 1 million units a year of Optimus robots in the current S, X space in Fremont. So that is slightly sad, but it is time to bring the S, X program to an end and shift really -- it's part of our overall shift to an autonomous future.

However, in my opinion, reality suggests that TSLA’s Optimus is facing significant competition from players who are already achieving commercial milestones that TSLA has only promised.

While TSLA has deployed over 1,000 Optimus units inside its own factories for learning and testing, it has yet to begin external commercial sales. In contrast, Agility Robotics’ Digit is already commercially deployed at Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and GXO Logistics, Inc. (GXO) warehouses. Similarly, Boston Dynamics launched its production-ready electric Atlas at CES 2026, with 2026 deployments already fully committed to Hyundai and GOOG's DeepMind.

The competitive landscape is further complicated by Chinese entrants like Unitree Robotics, which recently released the G1 humanoid for just $13,500. While Tesla aims for mass production at a price point of $20,000 to $30,000, competitors are already capturing the low-cost segments of the market.

Given that TSLA is behind both technologically and commercially, I fail to see the potential of Optimus & any justification for such a premium valuation. The market is currently valuing Optimus as a done deal, but for enterprise buyers in 2026, Agility’s Digit and Boston Dynamics’ Atlas are the robots with genuine commercial traction.

Unrealistic Economics of Terafab and Data Centers in Space

Recently, Elon Musk announced Terafab, a $20+ billion semiconductor manufacturing facility in Austin, Texas. This fab is expected to produce chips for TSLA’s EVs, robotaxis, and humanoid robots in addition to chips for data centers in space. Musk has set a target of one million wafer starts per month, which would represent roughly 70% of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s (TSM) entire global output.

As shown in the chart above, it is unrealistic to assume that TSLA can come anywhere close to developing a competitive enough fab, especially as a new entrant lacking the institutional knowledge many existing players have.

Even established giants like Intel Corporation (INTC) have struggled to maintain the conditions required to match TSMC’s yields. Semiconductor fabrication at 2nm requires ASML Holding N.V.’s (ASML) High-NA EUV lithography machines, which cost roughly $380 million each and have backlogs stretching years. Furthermore, TSLA’s institutional knowledge in chip design has seen significant attrition, including the departure of lead architect Peter Bannon in August 2025 after the Dojo supercomputer program was briefly canceled.

Additionally, from a financial standpoint, the market is ignoring the potential for a massive cash drain that could prolong payback periods and dilute short-term earnings quality as TSLA enters into a high-CapEx sector before its core profitability has stabilized. Many analysts have even said TSLA’s current projections for the costs of Terafab are incredibly conservative, with some analysts claiming that total capital investment can reach up to $45 billion.

Similarly speculative is the plan for orbital data centers, involving a million-satellite constellation generating 100 gigawatts of AI compute capacity. In a Tom’s Hardware article, Anton Shilov states the following:

“In GEO, building large AI data centers faces severe obstacles: megawatt-class GPU clusters would require enormous radiator wings to reject heat solely through infrared emission (as only radiative emission is possible, as Musk noted). This translates into tens of thousands of square meters of deployable structures per multi-gigawatt system, far beyond anything flown to date. Launching that mass would demand thousands of Starship-class flights, which is unrealistic within Musk's four-to-five-year window, and which is extremely expensive.”

Financial Performance

For FY2025, total revenue was reported at $94.8 billion, a 3% decline from 2024. Automotive revenue fell by 10% to $69.5 billion. On a quarter-on-quarter basis, Q4 2025 revenue showed an almost 13% decline.

I believe that the increase in free cash flow to $6.2 billion, while seemingly beneficial, was largely a result of temporarily reduced capital expenditures rather than real growth in operating income. While TSLA’s core automotive business is showing decline, its energy business, as previously mentioned, appears as the sole bright spot on TSLA’s financial statements.

TSLA’s delivery figures for Q4 2025 (418,227 units) represented a 16% year-over-year drop. This decline is particularly alarming when looking at the performance of competitor BYD Company Limited (BYDDF), which sold 2.25 million battery electric vehicles in 2025, surpassing Tesla as the world’s largest electric vehicle manufacturer.

Valuation

I retain the same valuation methodology I used in my previous article on TSLA. However, this is updated to reflect newer multiples from comparable companies and incorporates the latest TSLA earnings. Applying the average multiple of 16.06x of the selected comparable companies shown in the sheet above to TTM earnings of $3.794 billion and dividing by shares outstanding of 3.33 billion yields a price target of $16.25.

I would also like to show the degree to which TSLA is overvalued with a valuation exercise. Above are 4 staple big tech companies, alongside their current market cap and the current TTM P/E ratio they trade at. Under the new market cap column is their valuation assuming they traded at the same TTM P/E ratio as TSLA.

Assuming GOOG traded at the same P/E ratio as TSLA, it would be bigger than half the S&P 500, which itself has a market cap of $58.4 trillion. What is particularly surprising about this GOOG example is that GOOG operates robotaxis under Waymo, develops humanoid robotics, has developed a successful AI model (Gemini), operates data centers, and conducts cutting-edge research, yet it is a company that trades at a significantly cheaper multiple than TSLA.

Upside Risks

Musk has transitioned FSD to a subscription-only model, targeting 10 million active subscriptions. If adoption rates (currently 12% of the fleet) were to surge, it could provide a high-margin software revenue stream. However, this alone would not justify the valuation either.

Furthermore, Tesla remains a "story stock." As long as investors prioritize "future dreams" over "present production," the valuation may remain untethered from reality.

Conclusion

I believe that TSLA is trading at an extraordinarily high multiple that is hard to justify. To conclude, while TSLA’s energy business is excellent, its robotic humanoid ambitions do not live up to the hype and face considerable competition.
